evidence_quotes.quote: games such as "The Crew" or older games being rendered inoperable at the whims of a company

Games that are single-player shouldn't need an online connection and many indeed don't if the Games require an online connection self-hosting options should be provided for the game so that players can keep playing it even if the experience is diminished by the lack of other players
Instead of the sell of software by version or series many a software is now being sold exclusively as a license which is an enormous downgrade from what people expect or want
many people even had lifetime licenses taken from them

paragraphs.text: I will start by saying that the DSA can be interpreted to fulfill some of the points of this legislation and that enforcement of the DSA is important however the DSA is far too broad and lacks the SPECIFICITY and COMPREHENSION demanded by Roman Law. These prepositions are very important for the protection of the consumer but are also an important measure to protect an extensive medium and art-form that reflects a great portion of modern day society. The constant encroachments on our right to own propagated by software sellers has been a disgusting attack on our fundamental rights and freedoms and no country or union should stand for such a blatant example as this. For years buying meant ownership then they started saying that we didn't own anything that it was merely a license and ,some of you, ignored this as if it was an amorphous difference but, after seeing many a game being torn from a citizen's possession, one cannot accept such a distinction...games such as "The Crew" or older games being rendered inoperable at the whims of a company is an unprecedented assault on our rights and we cannot stand for this i should add that this reflects a bigger issue in the software market who has had several privileges on the back of it not representing a physical service or product. Instead of the sell of software by version or series many a software is now being sold exclusively as a license which is an enormous downgrade from what people expect or want...many people even had lifetime licenses taken from them...like in adobe products Games that are single-player shouldn't need an online connection and many indeed don't if the Games require an online connection self-hosting options should be provided for the game so that players can keep playing it even if the experience is diminished by the lack of other players The law being proposed here is good but it should go one step further, not only apply to all software that don't require a permanent online connection but also be far more demanding. Software as a piece of engineering has had a huge advantage over other pieces of engineering...you need but a glance at the motor of your car to see how it works...you can dismantle your car to see how it works...the same cannot be same for software and this represents a huge security issue both in the case of games but also in software used in many a office that could possibly hide malware, ransomware, PC melters, etc...and as such i propose that every single piece of publicly traded software should without a shadow of a doubt present their source code to be reviewed this could be done by a governmental agency but it would be preferential if the code could be reviewed by the public...though i admit this is a very code-left measure Developers shouldn't be threatened by this if they program things properly and securely, as copyright would still apply to their code regardless if people can see it or not. But returning to the original point the destruction of a game is in fact racial, cultural, ethnic erasure destroying not only a monumental cultural piece of art, engineering, and love but also possibly destroying the representation of certain minorities... For example when concord was deleted many plus sized Arabian lesbian women were dismayed as that was apparently their only representation within the gaming landscape It can alse be an erasure of national and historical identity...many games show in a new or artist light moments of history of several countries and their destruction could be considered lese Majeste...for example if a game has a Portuguese flag the deletion of the game could be considered deletion of that flag which is offence against Portugal.... This could also be seen as religious erasure by the same line of reasoning and a possible attack on every sort of identity... Erasing a game is an attack of our fundamental rights, our identities, our safety, our sovereignty, and our freedom
